schools.summary: The law of restitution for theft: 'if a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep' — the thief who stole and disposed of an ox or sheep (killing or selling it) to restore fivefold for the ox and fourfold for the sheep (the law of restitution, the theft made good with increase, the heavy penalty, the multiplied restitution, the deterrent against theft); the multiplied restitution — fivefold for the ox (the more useful beast, the loss of its labor), fourfold for the sheep (the witnesses noting the principle — restitution, the restoring of what was wrongfully taken, with increase, the eighth commandment enforced, Exodus 20:15, the wrong made good, not merely punished, the lesson — true repentance makes restitution, the thief turned honest restores, as Zacchaeus, 'if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold,' Luke 19:8, the gospel fruit of repentance, the restoring of ill-gotten gain, Ezekiel 33:15, 'if the wicked restore the pledge, give again that he had robbed,' the justice that makes the wrong good); the fivefold and fourfold (the heavy restitution, deterring theft, and compensating the loss) — the providence (the law's care for property, the wrong made good, the thief deterred) — Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the multiplied restitution teach?

sides.position: The theft made good with increase (the eighth commandment, Exodus 20:15) - the wrong restored not merely punished; the fruit of true repentance, the restoring of ill-gotten gain (Luke 19:8, Zacchaeus; Ezekiel 33:15); the justice that makes the wrong good and deters theft

themes: If a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep
the law of restitution for theft (the crux of making restitution)
the theft made good with increase, the eighth commandment enforced (Exodus 20:15)
the fruit of true repentance, the restoring of ill-gotten gain (Luke 19:8; Ezekiel 33:15)

schools.summary: The law of the thief slain in the night: 'if a thief be found breaking up, and be smitten that he die, there shall no blood be shed for him' — if a thief was caught breaking in (by night) and was struck and killed, no bloodguilt charged to the householder (the self-defense, the householder, unable to know the intruder's intent in the dark, not held guilty for killing him, the case, the nighttime burglar, the defender acquitted); the thief breaking in (by night, the burglar) — the case of the intruder (the danger in the dark); 'there shall no blood be shed for him' (no bloodguilt, the householder not punished for killing the night-intruder, who might mean murder as well as theft, the self-defense, the right to defend home and life against the burglar in the dark, the distinction, the night-thief, vv.3, the day-thief held differently, the light revealing intent, the providence, the law's equity, the defender acquitted where intent could not be known) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: If a thief be found breaking up, and be smitten that he die, there shall no blood be shed for him
the law of the thief slain in the night
the householder acquitted for killing the night-intruder whose intent could not be known
the right of self-defense against the burglar in the dark

schools.summary: The day-thief and the sale for restitution: 'if the sun be risen upon him, there shall be blood shed for him; for he should make full restitution; if he have nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft' — if the thief was killed by day (the sun risen, the intent visible, the householder able to see he meant only theft, not murder), there being bloodguilt (the householder not to kill for mere theft by day, the distinction, the night-thief, v.2, from the day-thief, the light revealing intent, the value of life, the thief not to be killed for theft alone, where no danger to life); the full restitution (the thief to restore, not to be killed, by day) — the just penalty (restitution, not death, for theft, the law's measure); 'if he have nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft' (the insolvent thief sold, into servitude, to make the restitution from his labor, the justice, the debt of restitution paid by his service, the limited bondage, Exodus 21:2, the providence, the wrong made good, the thief bound to restore by his labor) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: If the sun be risen upon him, there shall be blood shed for him; for he should make full restitution; if he have nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft
the day-thief and the sale for restitution
the value of life over property, the thief not killed for mere theft by day
the wrong made good by restitution or service (Exodus 21:2)

schools.summary: Double restitution for the live theft: 'if the theft be certainly found in his hand alive, whether it be ox, or ass, or sheep; he shall restore double' — if the stolen animal was found alive in the thief's possession (not yet killed or sold), he restoring double (only twofold, less than the fivefold and fourfold of v.1, since he had not disposed of it, the graded restitution, double for the live theft, more for the disposed, the justice, the penalty proportioned to the degree of the offense); the stolen beast found alive (in the thief's hand) — the lesser offense (not yet sold or killed, the case); 'he shall restore double' (the double restitution, twofold, for the live theft, the graded penalty, less than the fivefold/fourfold, since the theft was not completed by sale or slaughter, the providence, the equity of the law, the penalty proportioned to the offense) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: If the theft be certainly found in his hand alive, whether it be ox, or ass, or sheep; he shall restore double
double restitution for the live theft
the graded penalty - double for the live theft, fivefold for the disposed (v.1)
the penalty proportioned to the degree of the offense

schools.summary: Restitution for trespass by grazing: 'if a man shall cause a field or vineyard to be eaten, and shall put in his beast, and shall feed in another man's field; of the best of his own field, and of the best of his own vineyard, shall he make restitution' — if a man let his beast graze in another's field or vineyard (damaging the crop), he must make restitution from the best of his own field or vineyard (the trespass, the damage to the neighbour's crop by negligent or willful grazing, the restitution, from the best, the full and generous compensation); the beast grazing in another's field (the damage) — the trespass (the harm to the neighbour); 'of the best of his own field, and of the best of his own vineyard, shall he make restitution' (the restitution from the best, not the worst, but the best of the offender's own, the generous compensation, the wrong made good fully, even handsomely, the providence, the equity of the law, the neighbour's loss made good from the best) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: If a man shall cause a field or vineyard to be eaten, and shall put in his beast, and shall feed in another man's field; of the best of his own field... shall he make restitution
restitution for trespass by grazing
the damage to the neighbour's crop made good
from the best of the offender's own, full and generous compensation

schools.summary: Restitution for damage by fire: 'if fire break out, and catch in thorns, so that the stacks of corn, or the standing corn, or the field, be consumed therewith; he that kindled the fire shall surely make restitution' — if a man kindled a fire (to burn thorns or stubble) which spread and consumed a neighbour's corn (stacked or standing) or field, he must make restitution (the negligence, the fire let spread, the careless kindling, the liability, the kindler responsible for the damage his fire caused); the fire spreading (from thorns to the corn) — the damage (the careless kindling); 'he that kindled the fire shall surely make restitution' (the kindler liable, for the damage his fire caused, even if it spread beyond his intent, the responsibility, the duty of care with fire, the kindler accountable, the providence, the law's care for the neighbour's property, negligence with fire punished) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: If fire break out, and catch in thorns, so that the stacks of corn, or the standing corn, or the field, be consumed therewith; he that kindled the fire shall surely make restitution
restitution for damage by fire
the kindler liable for the damage his fire caused
the duty of care with fire, negligence punished

schools.summary: The law of the deposit and the thief's double restitution: 'if a man shall deliver unto his neighbour money or stuff to keep, and it be stolen out of the man's house; if the thief be found, let him pay double' — if goods (money or stuff) entrusted to a neighbour for safekeeping were stolen, and the thief was found, the thief paying double (the trust, the deposit, the bailment, the goods committed to a keeper, the restitution, the thief, if found, paying double, the keeper not liable if the theft was not his fault); the deposit (goods entrusted to a neighbour) — the trust (the duty of the keeper, to guard faithfully, vv.8); 'if the thief be found, let him pay double' (the thief paying double, if found, the restitution for the theft, the justice, the thief liable, the keeper cleared if faithful, the providence, the law's care for trust and property, the thief punished, the honest keeper protected) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: If a man shall deliver unto his neighbour money or stuff to keep, and it be stolen out of the man's house; if the thief be found, let him pay double
the law of the deposit and the thief's double restitution
the goods entrusted to a keeper, the duty to guard faithfully
the thief liable, the honest keeper cleared

schools.summary: The keeper brought before the judges if the thief is not found: 'if the thief be not found, then the master of the house shall be brought unto the judges, to see whether he have put his hand unto his neighbour's goods' — if the thief was not found, the keeper (master of the house) brought before the judges (God, the magistrates) to determine whether he himself had taken the entrusted goods (the inquiry, the keeper examined, to clear or convict him, the oath, the keeper, if innocent, clearing himself by oath before God, vv.11, the justice, the truth sought where the thief was unknown); the keeper brought before the judges (to be examined) — the inquiry (the truth sought); 'to see whether he have put his hand unto his neighbour's goods' (the examination, whether the keeper himself was the thief, the oath before God, the keeper clearing himself, vv.11, the providence, the law's care for justice, the truth sought, the innocent cleared, the guilty exposed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: If the thief be not found, then the master of the house shall be brought unto the judges, to see whether he have put his hand unto his neighbour's goods
the keeper brought before the judges if the thief is not found
the keeper examined to clear or convict him, the oath before God (vv.11)
the truth sought where the thief was unknown

schools.summary: The law of disputed ownership settled by the judges: 'for all manner of trespass, whether it be for ox, for ass, for sheep, for raiment, or for any manner of lost thing, which another challengeth to be his, the cause of both parties shall come before the judges; and whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour' — in all disputes of ownership (ox, ass, sheep, raiment, lost thing), where one claimed another's possession, both parties to bring their cause before the judges, and whoever the judges condemned (as the wrongful claimant or holder) paying double (the recourse to the judges, the dispute settled by lawful authority, not by force, the justice, the condemned paying double); both parties before the judges (the dispute settled by the magistrates) — the lawful recourse (the appeal to authority, Deuteronomy 1:17, the judges as God's ministers, Romans 13:1-4); 'whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour' (the condemned paying double, the penalty for the wrongful claim or holding, the justice, the wrong made good, the false claimant punished, the providence, the law's order for settling disputes, the judges deciding, the wrong restored) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: For all manner of trespass... which another challengeth to be his, the cause of both parties shall come before the judges; and whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour
the law of disputed ownership settled by the judges
the dispute settled by lawful authority, not by force (Deuteronomy 1:17)
the condemned paying double, the wrong made good

schools.summary: The law of the entrusted beast that dies or is lost: 'if a man deliver unto his neighbour an ass, or an ox, or a sheep, or any beast, to keep; and it die, or be hurt, or driven away, no man seeing it' — if a beast entrusted to a neighbour (for keeping) died, was hurt, or driven away (stolen), with no witness (the trust, the beast committed to a keeper, the case, the loss without witness, where the keeper's fault could not be proved, the question of liability, settled by oath, vv.11); the beast entrusted (to a keeper) — the trust (the bailment); 'it die, or be hurt, or driven away, no man seeing it' (the loss without witness, death, injury, or theft, unseen, the case, the keeper's fault unproven, the providence, the law's provision for the case, the oath to settle it, vv.11) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: If a man deliver unto his neighbour an ass, or an ox, or a sheep, or any beast, to keep; and it die, or be hurt, or driven away, no man seeing it
the law of the entrusted beast that dies or is lost
the loss without witness, the keeper's fault unproven
the matter settled by oath (vv.11)

schools.summary: The oath of the LORD settling the unwitnessed case: 'then shall an oath of the LORD be between them both, that he hath not put his hand unto his neighbour's goods; and the owner of it shall accept thereof, and he shall not make it good' — where the loss was unwitnessed (v.10), the keeper to swear an 'oath of the LORD' (a solemn oath in God's name) that he had not taken the goods nor been negligent (the oath, the appeal to God, who sees the truth, as the final settlement where no witness could decide, the lawful oath, the solemn swearing by God's name to end the dispute, Hebrews 6:16, 'an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife'); the oath of the LORD (the solemn, lawful oath, sworn in God's name) — the lawful use of the oath (to settle truth where man cannot, Deuteronomy 6:13, the third commandment not forbidding the reverent oath, but the vain, the appeal to God who knows the heart); 'the owner of it shall accept thereof, and he shall not make it good' (the owner accepting the oath, resting in it, the keeper acquitted, not making restitution, since the loss was not his fault, the justice, the honest keeper cleared by the oath, the providence, the matter ended by the appeal to God, the truth committed to him who sees all) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Then shall an oath of the LORD be between them both, that he hath not put his hand unto his neighbour's goods; and the owner of it shall accept thereof, and he shall not make it good
the oath of the LORD settling the unwitnessed case
the lawful oath, the appeal to God who sees the truth (Deuteronomy 6:13; Hebrews 6:16)
the honest keeper cleared, the dispute ended

schools.summary: The keeper liable if the beast is stolen through his neglect: 'and if it be stolen from him, he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof' — if the entrusted beast was stolen (through the keeper's negligence, as distinct from the unwitnessed loss of v.11), the keeper must make restitution (the liability, the keeper accountable for theft he could have prevented, the distinction, the unwitnessed loss, cleared by oath, v.11, from the theft through negligence, the keeper liable); the beast stolen (from the keeper, through his fault or lack of care) — the negligence (the liability); 'he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof' (the keeper restoring the value, for the theft he should have prevented, the justice, the negligent keeper liable, the providence, the law's care for trust, the keeper held to faithful guarding) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if it be stolen from him, he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof
the keeper liable if the beast is stolen through his neglect
the keeper accountable for theft he should have prevented
the duty of faithful keeping enforced

schools.summary: The keeper cleared if the beast is torn by a wild beast: 'if it be torn in pieces, then let him bring it for witness, and he shall not make good that which was torn' — if the entrusted beast was torn by a wild beast (killed by a predator, beyond the keeper's power to prevent), the keeper to bring the torn carcass (or remains) as evidence, and he not liable (the proof, the torn remains as witness to the manner of loss, the acquittal, the keeper cleared where the loss was not his fault, the predator's work, Amos 3:12; Genesis 31:39, Jacob bearing the loss of the torn); the torn carcass brought as witness (the evidence of the predator's work) — the proof (the keeper's defense); 'he shall not make good that which was torn' (the keeper not liable, for the loss by the wild beast, beyond his power, the justice, the keeper cleared where the loss was unavoidable, the providence, the law's equity, the keeper not charged for what he could not prevent, Genesis 31:39; Amos 3:12) — JFB, Gill.

themes: If it be torn in pieces, then let him bring it for witness, and he shall not make good that which was torn
the keeper cleared if the beast is torn by a wild beast
the torn carcass brought as witness to the manner of loss
the keeper not charged for the unavoidable loss (Genesis 31:39; Amos 3:12)

schools.summary: The borrower liable for the borrowed beast hurt or dead: 'and if a man borrow ought of his neighbour, and it be hurt, or die, the owner thereof being not with it, he shall surely make it good' — if a man borrowed something (a beast) from his neighbour, and it was hurt or died while in his use (the owner not present), the borrower must make it good (the liability, the borrower fully responsible, having the use and benefit, the distinction, the borrower, who uses the thing for his own profit, liable, unlike the keeper of a deposit, vv.10-13); the borrowed beast hurt or dying (in the borrower's use) — the case (the liability of the borrower); 'the owner thereof being not with it, he shall surely make it good' (the borrower liable, the owner not present, the borrower bearing the risk of what he borrowed for his own use, the justice, the borrower, who had the benefit, bearing the loss, the providence, the equity of the law, the borrower responsible for the borrowed thing) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if a man borrow ought of his neighbour, and it be hurt, or die, the owner thereof being not with it, he shall surely make it good
the borrower liable for the borrowed beast hurt or dead
the borrower fully responsible, having the use and benefit
the borrower bearing the loss, unlike the keeper of a deposit (vv.10-13)

schools.summary: The exceptions for the owner present and the hired beast: 'but if the owner thereof be with it, he shall not make it good: if it be an hired thing, it came for his hire' — if the owner was present (with his beast, e.g. hired along with it, able to guard it himself), the borrower not liable, and if it was a hired thing (rented for a fee), the loss coming 'for his hire' (the hire-fee covering the risk, the owner bearing the loss as part of the bargain, the exceptions, the owner present, or the hired thing, the borrower not liable); the owner present (guarding his own) — the borrower cleared (the owner bearing the risk when present); 'if it be an hired thing, it came for his hire' (the hired beast, the loss covered by the hire-fee, the owner bearing the risk as part of the rental, the justice, the hire including the risk, the providence, the equity of the law, the cases justly distinguished, the borrower, the owner, and the hirer each bearing their proper share) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But if the owner thereof be with it, he shall not make it good: if it be an hired thing, it came for his hire
the exceptions for the owner present and the hired beast
the owner present bears the risk, the hire-fee covers the loss
the cases justly distinguished, each bearing their proper share

schools.summary: The law of the seduced virgin: 'and if a man entice a maid that is not betrothed, and lie with her, he shall surely endow her to be his wife' — if a man seduced (enticed) an unbetrothed virgin and lay with her, he was bound to endow her (pay the dowry) and take her to wife (the seduction, the wrong done to the maid and her family, the obligation, the seducer to marry and endow her, or pay the dowry, vv.17, the protection of the maid, her honor and future secured, the seducer held responsible, Deuteronomy 22:28-29); the enticing of the unbetrothed maid (the seduction) — the wrong (the dishonor done); 'he shall surely endow her to be his wife' (the seducer bound to marry and endow her, the dowry paid, the maid taken to wife, or the dowry paid if refused, vv.17, the protection, the maid's honor and future secured, the law's care for the wronged, the providence, the seventh commandment, Exodus 20:14, guarding chastity, the seducer held to responsibility) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And if a man entice a maid that is not betrothed, and lie with her, he shall surely endow her to be his wife
the law of the seduced virgin
the seducer bound to marry and endow her (Deuteronomy 22:28-29)
the maid's honor and future secured, chastity guarded (Exodus 20:14)

schools.summary: The dowry paid if the father refuses the marriage: 'if her father utterly refuse to give her unto him, he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins' — if the maid's father refused to give her in marriage to the seducer (unwilling for the match), the seducer must still pay the dowry of virgins (the bride-price, the protection of the maid, the dowry paid even if the marriage was refused, the maid not left wronged and unprovided, the father's right, to refuse the match, yet the seducer still penalized); the father's refusal (unwilling to give his daughter to the seducer) — the father's authority (the right to refuse); 'he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins' (the dowry paid, the bride-price, even without the marriage, the protection, the maid compensated, the seducer penalized, the providence, the law's care for the wronged maid, the seducer held responsible whether or not the marriage followed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Wesley, Gill.

themes: If her father utterly refuse to give her unto him, he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins
the dowry paid if the father refuses the marriage
the father's right to refuse the match, the seducer still penalized
the maid compensated, the law's care for the wronged

schools.summary: The law against witchcraft: 'thou shalt not suffer a witch to live' — the command — 'Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live': the witch (the sorceress, the practiser of magic, divination, dealing with familiar spirits) not to be allowed to live (the witnesses noting the meaning — the 'witch,' Hebrew mekashshephah, the female practiser of sorcery, magic arts, dealing with evil spirits, casting spells, poisoning, divining, the capital condemnation, witchcraft a capital crime, Leviticus 20:27; Deuteronomy 18:10-12, 'there shall not be found among you... a witch, or a charmer, or a consulter with familiar spirits,' the reason — witchcraft a rebellion against God, the seeking of forbidden powers, the trafficking with evil spirits, the rejection of God for the devil, 1 Samuel 15:23, 'rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft'; 28, the witch of Endor, the abomination, the dark arts, the deception of the people, the dishonor of God, the lesson — the deadly seriousness of dabbling in the occult, the works of darkness condemned, Galatians 5:20, 'witchcraft' among the works of the flesh; Acts 19:19, the burning of the magic books; Revelation 21:8; 22:15, sorcerers shut out); the severity (the witch not suffered to live) — the gravity of the crime (the trafficking with evil spirits, the deception, the rebellion against God, the protection of the people, from the deadly deception of sorcery) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: Why was the witch not to be suffered to live?

sides.position: The capital condemnation of witchcraft (Leviticus 20:27; Deuteronomy 18:10-12) - the sorcery and trafficking with evil spirits a rebellion against God (1 Samuel 15:23), an abomination, the works of darkness condemned (Galatians 5:20; Acts 19:19; Revelation 21:8); the deadly seriousness of the occult

themes: Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live
the law against witchcraft (the crux of the death of the witch)
the capital condemnation of sorcery and trafficking with evil spirits (Leviticus 20:27; Deuteronomy 18:10-12)
witchcraft a rebellion against God, the works of darkness condemned (1 Samuel 15:23; Galatians 5:20; Revelation 21:8)

schools.summary: The death penalty for bestiality: 'whosoever lieth with a beast shall surely be put to death' — whoever committed bestiality (the unnatural union of man and beast) to be put to death (the abomination, the perversion, the confusion of the orders of creation, the defilement, Leviticus 18:23; 20:15-16, the capital crime, the deadly sin, the corruption of nature, the sanctity of the created order, the perversion judged with death); the bestiality (the unnatural, abominable union) — the perversion (the defilement, Leviticus 18:23); 'shall surely be put to death' (the death penalty, the abomination judged, the perversion of nature, Leviticus 20:15-16, the sanctity of the created order, the confusion of kinds condemned, the providence, the law guarding the orders of creation, the perversion judged) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Whosoever lieth with a beast shall surely be put to death
the death penalty for bestiality
the abomination, the confusion of the orders of creation (Leviticus 18:23; 20:15-16)
the sanctity of the created order, the perversion judged

schools.summary: The death penalty for idolatrous sacrifice: 'he that sacrificeth unto any god, save unto the LORD only, he shall be utterly destroyed' — whoever sacrificed to any god but the LORD to be utterly destroyed (devoted to destruction, the herem, the idolatry, the worship of false gods, the breach of the first commandment, Exodus 20:3, the capital crime, apostasy from the LORD, the giving of God's worship to another, the jealousy of God, who would not share his glory, Isaiah 42:8); the sacrificing to false gods (the idolatry) — the apostasy (the breach of the first commandment, Exodus 20:3); 'he shall be utterly destroyed' (the utter destruction, the herem, devoted to destruction, Deuteronomy 13:6-18, the jealousy of God, the LORD alone to be worshipped, Exodus 20:3-5; 34:14, the providence, the law guarding the worship of the one God, idolatry judged with death) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: He that sacrificeth unto any god, save unto the LORD only, he shall be utterly destroyed
the death penalty for idolatrous sacrifice
the apostasy, the breach of the first commandment (Exodus 20:3)
the jealousy of God, the LORD alone to be worshipped (Deuteronomy 13:6-18)

schools.summary: The care of the stranger grounded in Israel's own bondage: 'thou shalt neither vex a stranger, nor oppress him: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt' — Israel not to vex (wrong, mistreat) or oppress the stranger (the foreigner, the sojourner among them, the care of the stranger, the vulnerable foreigner protected, the prohibition of oppression, the stranger not to be wronged in word or deed, Leviticus 19:33-34); the stranger protected (not to be vexed or oppressed) — the care for the foreigner (the love of the stranger, Leviticus 19:34, 'love him as thyself'); the motive — 'for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt': the ground of the command, Israel's own experience as oppressed strangers in Egypt (the lesson — those who have known oppression should not oppress, the memory of their own bondage moving them to compassion, Deuteronomy 10:19, 'love ye therefore the stranger: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt,' the golden rule rooted in experience, doing to the stranger as they would have been done by, the compassion of the redeemed, those delivered from bondage tender to the bound, the care for the alien a mark of true religion, the stranger, with the widow and fatherless, the special object of God's care and the test of justice, Malachi 3:5; Zechariah 7:10, the love that welcomes the stranger, Matthew 25:35, 'I was a stranger, and ye took me in') — Keil and Delitzsch, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What is the ground of the care for the stranger?

sides.position: Israel's own experience as oppressed strangers in Egypt (Deuteronomy 10:19) - those who have known oppression should not oppress; the compassion of the redeemed who knew bondage, the love of the stranger a mark of true religion (Leviticus 19:33-34; Malachi 3:5; Matthew 25:35)

themes: Thou shalt neither vex a stranger, nor oppress him: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt
the care of the stranger grounded in Israel's own bondage
the care for the vulnerable foreigner (Leviticus 19:33-34)
grounded in Israel's own experience of oppression, a mark of true religion (Deuteronomy 10:19; Matthew 25:35)

schools.summary: The care of the widow and the fatherless: 'ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child' — the charge — 'Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child': Israel forbidden to afflict (wrong, mistreat, oppress) the widow or the fatherless (the witnesses noting the special care — the widow and the orphan, the most helpless and defenseless, having lost husband and father, their natural protectors, the special objects of God's care and the test of true religion, the lesson — God himself the protector of the widow and fatherless, the 'father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows,' Psalm 68:5, the LORD who 'relieveth the fatherless and widow,' Psalm 146:9, who 'doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow,' Deuteronomy 10:18, the care for them a mark of pure religion, 'Pure religion and undefiled before God... is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction,' James 1:27, the oppression of them a crying sin, provoking God's vengeance, vv.23-24; Malachi 3:5; Zechariah 7:10; Isaiah 1:17, 23); the widow and fatherless protected (the helpless not to be afflicted) — the special care for the defenseless (the duty of justice and mercy to them); the widow and orphan — the most vulnerable, under God's special care (Psalm 68:5; James 1:27, the providence, God the avenger of the wronged helpless, vv.23-24, the test of true religion the care for them) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: Why the special charge concerning the widow and fatherless?

sides.position: They are the most helpless and defenseless (having lost their natural protectors), the special objects of God's care - God himself the protector of the widow and fatherless (Psalm 68:5; 146:9; Deuteronomy 10:18); the care for them a mark of pure religion (James 1:27), the oppression of them a crying sin provoking God's vengeance (vv.23-24)

themes: Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child
the care of the widow and the fatherless (the crux of God's protection of the helpless)
the most defenseless under God's own protection (Psalm 68:5; 146:9; Deuteronomy 10:18)
the care for them a mark of pure religion (James 1:27), their oppression a crying sin

schools.summary: God hearing the cry of the afflicted helpless: 'if thou afflict them in any wise, and they cry at all unto me, I will surely hear their cry' — if Israel afflicted the widow or fatherless, and they cried to God, God would surely hear their cry (the appeal to God, the helpless, having no human protector, crying to God their defender, the certainty, God surely hearing the cry of the oppressed, the warning, the affliction of the helpless brings God's avenging notice); the affliction (in any way, any wrong to the helpless) — the warning (the sin that God marks); 'they cry at all unto me, I will surely hear their cry' (the cry of the helpless heard by God, their defender, who hears the cry of the oppressed, as he heard Israel's in Egypt, Exodus 3:7; Psalm 9:12, 'he forgetteth not the cry of the humble'; James 5:4, the cry of the defrauded laborer, the certainty of God's hearing, the avenger of the wronged, the providence, God the protector of the helpless, their cry coming up before him) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: If thou afflict them in any wise, and they cry at all unto me, I will surely hear their cry
God hearing the cry of the afflicted helpless
the helpless heard by God their defender, as Israel's cry in Egypt (Exodus 3:7)
God the avenger of the wronged (Psalm 9:12; James 5:4)

schools.summary: God's wrath against the oppressor of the helpless: 'and my wrath shall wax hot, and I will kill you with the sword; and your wives shall be widows, and your children fatherless' — God threatening that his wrath would burn against the oppressor of the widow and fatherless, and he would slay him with the sword, making his own wife a widow and his children fatherless (the just retribution, the oppressor measured with his own measure, the wrong he did to others done to his own, the terror of God's wrath, the avenger of the helpless, the warning, the oppression of the defenseless a sin that kindles God's anger); God's hot wrath (against the oppressor, the sword of judgment) — the avenging anger (the terror of the threat); 'your wives shall be widows, and your children fatherless' (the just measure, the oppressor's own family made widows and orphans, as he made others, the retribution, the wrong returned on his own head, Matthew 7:2, 'with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again,' the providence, God the avenger of the helpless, the oppressor judged in kind) — Gill.

themes: And my wrath shall wax hot, and I will kill you with the sword; and your wives shall be widows, and your children fatherless
God's wrath against the oppressor of the helpless
the oppressor measured with his own measure, judged in kind (Matthew 7:2)
God the avenger of the helpless

schools.summary: The law against usury to the poor: 'if thou lend money to any of my people that is poor by thee, thou shalt not be to him as an usurer, neither shalt thou lay upon him usury' — the charge — 'thou shalt not be to him as an usurer, neither shalt thou lay upon him usury': when lending money to the poor among God's people, Israel not to act as a usurer (a hard moneylender, exacting interest), nor to lay usury (interest) upon the poor (the witnesses noting the meaning and limit — the prohibition is of usury to the poor, the taking of interest from the needy brother, grinding the poor with the loan that should relieve him, the lesson — the duty of mercy in lending, lending to the poor freely, to relieve, not to profit from their distress, Leviticus 25:35-37; Deuteronomy 23:19-20; Psalm 15:5, 'he that putteth not out his money to usury'; Ezekiel 18:8, the witnesses noting the careful distinction — not all interest, in ordinary commerce, between those able to bear it, is condemned, but the oppressive exaction from the poor, the abuse of the needy, the love that lends 'hoping for nothing again,' Luke 6:34-35, 'lend, hoping for nothing again,' the care for the poor brother, the loan a means of mercy, not of gain); the duty of mercy (lending to the poor without usury, to relieve their need) — the compassion (the care for the poor brother, Leviticus 25:35-37); 'any of my people that is poor by thee' (the poor brother, the special object of the mercy, the prohibition of grinding the poor with interest, the providence, the law's care for the poor, the loan a means of relief, not of oppression) — Calvin, Henry, Wesley, Gill, JFB.

debates.question: Does this forbid all interest, or oppressive usury to the poor?

sides.position: The prohibition is of oppressive interest exacted from the poor brother (Leviticus 25:35-37; Deuteronomy 23:19-20; Psalm 15:5) - the duty of merciful lending to relieve, not to profit from, the needy (Luke 6:34-35); the witnesses carefully distinguish oppressive usury to the poor from ordinary interest in commerce

themes: If thou lend money to any of my people that is poor by thee, thou shalt not be to him as an usurer, neither shalt thou lay upon him usury
the law against usury to the poor (the crux of mercy in lending)
the prohibition of grinding the poor with interest (Leviticus 25:35-37; Deuteronomy 23:19-20; Psalm 15:5)
the duty of merciful lending to relieve the needy brother (Luke 6:34-35)

schools.summary: The returning of the poor man's pledged garment by night: 'if thou at all take thy neighbour's raiment to pledge, thou shalt deliver it unto him by that the sun goeth down' — if a poor man's garment (his outer cloak, used also as his blanket) was taken as a pledge (security for a loan), it must be returned to him by sundown (the mercy, the poor man not deprived of his covering for the night, the limit on the creditor, the pledge not to leave the poor man without his necessary garment, Deuteronomy 24:10-13); the garment taken as security (the pledge) — the loan secured (the poor man's cloak); 'thou shalt deliver it unto him by that the sun goeth down' (the garment returned by sundown, that the poor man might have his covering for the night, vv.27, the mercy, the creditor's right limited by the poor man's need, the providence, the law's tender care for the poor, the necessary covering not withheld, Deuteronomy 24:12-13, 'thou shalt deliver him the pledge... that he may sleep in his own raiment, and bless thee') — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If thou at all take thy neighbour's raiment to pledge, thou shalt deliver it unto him by that the sun goeth down
the returning of the poor man's pledged garment by night
the poor man not deprived of his covering for the night (Deuteronomy 24:10-13)
the creditor's right limited by the poor man's need

schools.summary: The reason of the mercy and God's graciousness to the poor: 'for that is his covering only, it is his raiment for his skin: wherein shall he sleep? and it shall come to pass, when he crieth unto me, that I will hear; for I am gracious' — the poor man's garment his only covering, his raiment next his skin, in which he must sleep (the reason of the mercy, the poor man's necessary covering, without which he could not rest, the tender care, the law providing for the poor man's basic need, the compassion); the poor man's need of his garment to sleep (his only covering) — the basic necessity (the ground of the mercy); 'when he crieth unto me, that I will hear; for I am gracious' (the poor man's cry heard by God, his defender, and the ground of it — 'I am gracious,' the character of God, gracious, compassionate, the defender of the poor, Psalm 86:15, the hearer of the needy's cry, vv.23, the one whose grace is the pattern and the motive for mercy to the poor, the providence, God gracious to the poor, hearing their cry, the oppressor of them answerable to him, the mercy of God the ground of mercy in his people, Luke 6:36, 'Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful') — Gill.

themes: For that is his covering only, it is his raiment for his skin: wherein shall he sleep? and it shall come to pass, when he crieth unto me, that I will hear; for I am gracious
the reason of the mercy and God's graciousness to the poor
the poor man's only covering, his basic need honored
God gracious to the poor, his mercy the pattern for ours (Psalm 86:15; Luke 6:36)

schools.summary: The law against reviling rulers and judges: 'thou shalt not revile the gods, nor curse the ruler of thy people' — the 'gods' (Hebrew elohim) here, most witnesses hold, the judges and magistrates (called 'gods' as God's representatives, exercising his authority in judgment, Psalm 82:1, 6, 'I have said, Ye are gods'; John 10:34-35), Israel not to revile (speak evil of, despise) the judges, nor curse the ruler (the honor of authority, the magistrates and rulers, as God's ministers, to be respected, not reviled, the order of society, the dishonor of rulers a breach of the order God ordained); the 'gods' as the judges (the magistrates, God's representatives in judgment, Psalm 82:6) — not to be reviled (the respect for authority, the office honored, though the man be fallible); 'nor curse the ruler of thy people' (the ruler, the prince, the magistrate, not to be cursed, the honor of authority, the powers ordained of God, Romans 13:1-7; 1 Peter 2:17, 'honour the king,' the New Testament citing — Paul, having unwittingly reviled the high priest, owning this law: 'I wist not, brethren, that he was the high priest: for it is written, Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people,' Acts 23:5, the providence, the respect for the office of authority, even when the man is faulty, the order of society guarded) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Clarke.

debates.question: Who are the 'gods' that are not to be reviled?

sides.position: The judges and magistrates, called 'gods' as God's representatives exercising his authority in judgment (Psalm 82:1, 6; John 10:34-35) - the honor due to God-ordained authority, the office respected though the man be fallible; cited by Paul of the high priest (Acts 23:5; Romans 13:1-7)

themes: Thou shalt not revile the gods, nor curse the ruler of thy people
the law against reviling rulers and judges
the 'gods' the judges, God's representatives in judgment (Psalm 82:6; John 10:34-35)
the honor due to God-ordained authority, cited by Paul (Acts 23:5; Romans 13:1-7)

schools.summary: The firstfruits and the firstborn owed to God: 'thou shalt not delay to offer the first of thy ripe fruits, and of thy liquors: the firstborn of thy sons shalt thou give unto me' — Israel not to delay offering the firstfruits (of the harvest, the grain and the wine/oil) to God, and the firstborn of their sons to be given to him (the firstfruits, the first and best given to God, Proverbs 3:9, 'honour the LORD with thy substance, and with the firstfruits,' the promptness, no delay in rendering God his due); the firstfruits (the first of the harvest and vintage, given promptly to God) — the acknowledgment of God as the giver (the first and best his due); 'the firstborn of thy sons shalt thou give unto me' (the firstborn of the sons given to God, consecrated, then redeemed, Exodus 13:2, 13; 34:20; Numbers 18:15-16, the claim of God on the firstborn, his by the sparing in Egypt, Exodus 13:15, the lesson — the first and best, the firstfruits and firstborn, the LORD's, a type of Christ the firstborn, the firstfruits, 1 Corinthians 15:20, 23; Colossians 1:18, and the consecration of the redeemed as firstfruits to God, James 1:18; Revelation 14:4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott.

themes: Thou shalt not delay to offer the first of thy ripe fruits, and of thy liquors: the firstborn of thy sons shalt thou give unto me
the firstfruits and the firstborn owed to God
the first and best the LORD's, given promptly (Proverbs 3:9; Exodus 13:2)
a type of Christ the firstborn and firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:20; Colossians 1:18)

schools.summary: The firstborn of the cattle given to God on the eighth day: 'likewise shalt thou do with thine oxen, and with thy sheep: seven days it shall be with his dam; on the eighth day thou shalt give it me' — the firstborn of the oxen and sheep likewise to be given to God, after remaining seven days with its dam (mother), given on the eighth day (the firstborn of the cattle, the LORD's, as the firstborn of the sons, the seven days, the young left with its mother, then given, as the child circumcised on the eighth day, Leviticus 22:27, the consecration of the firstborn beasts); the seven days with the dam (the young not taken too soon) — the mercy and order (the time appointed); 'on the eighth day thou shalt give it me' (the firstborn beast given on the eighth day, consecrated to God, as the firstborn sons, the claim of God on the firstborn, the first and best his, v.29, the providence, the firstfruits and firstborn the LORD's, the eighth-day giving paralleling the circumcision, Leviticus 12:3, a type of the consecration of the first and best to God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Likewise shalt thou do with thine oxen, and with thy sheep: seven days it shall be with his dam; on the eighth day thou shalt give it me
the firstborn of the cattle given to God on the eighth day
the firstborn beasts the LORD's, as the firstborn sons (v.29)
the eighth-day giving paralleling the circumcision (Leviticus 22:27; 12:3)

schools.summary: The call to holiness and the law against torn flesh: 'and ye shall be holy men unto me: neither shall ye eat any flesh that is torn of beasts in the field; ye shall cast it to the dogs' — Israel called to be holy men unto God (consecrated, set apart), and as a mark of that holiness, not to eat flesh torn by wild beasts (the carcass with its blood, unclean), but to cast it to the dogs (the call to holiness, the people set apart unto God, the ground of the particular laws, Leviticus 11:44-45; 19:2, the separation, the holy people distinguished even in their food); the call to holiness (the people consecrated unto God) — the consecration (the mark of the holy nation, Exodus 19:6; 1 Peter 1:15-16, 'be ye holy; for I am holy'); 'neither shall ye eat any flesh that is torn of beasts in the field; ye shall cast it to the dogs' (the torn flesh forbidden, the carcass with its blood, the unclean, Leviticus 17:15; 22:8, the holiness, the people distinguished even in diet, the separation from the common and unclean, the lesson, the holiness of God's people in all things, the call to be holy as God is holy, 1 Peter 1:15-16; 2 Corinthians 6:17, 'come out from among them, and be ye separate,' the providence, the people set apart unto God, holiness the mark of the redeemed) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And ye shall be holy men unto me: neither shall ye eat any flesh that is torn of beasts in the field; ye shall cast it to the dogs
the call to holiness and the law against torn flesh
the people set apart unto God, holy as God is holy (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:15-16)
the holy people distinguished even in their food (Leviticus 17:15; 2 Corinthians 6:17)
